shortDescription: Rebuild every damaged or missing tooth across both arches in Korea using implants, crowns, bridges and digital dentistry over a multi-trip plan.

sections.overview: Full mouth reconstruction, also called full mouth rehabilitation, is a coordinated treatment plan that restores or rebuilds nearly all of the teeth across both the upper and lower jaws under a single strategy. It is not one procedure but a sequence of treatments tailored to each patient, typically combining dental implants, crowns, bridges, periodontal (gum) therapy and, when needed, bone grafting or orthodontic alignment. The goal is to restore chewing function, correct the bite, eliminate disease and improve appearance when teeth have been lost or extensively damaged by decay, gum disease, trauma, wear or congenital conditions. In South Korea, full mouth reconstruction has become a leading reason patients travel from across Africa and elsewhere, drawn by the combination of advanced digital planning, experienced prosthodontists and surgeons, and costs well below those in many Western countries. A Korean clinic usually begins with detailed imaging and a written plan that maps out every step, the order of treatment and an estimated timeline before any work starts. Because the process restores both arches and may involve healing periods for implants to fuse with bone, it is inherently staged and almost always spread across more than one clinic visit. For international patients this means the journey is planned around two or more trips to Korea, with remote follow-up in between, rather than a single appointment.
sections.candidacy: Full mouth reconstruction is intended for people with widespread dental problems rather than a single damaged tooth. Good candidates often include those who have lost many or all of their teeth, have severe decay or fractures across multiple teeth, suffer from advanced gum disease that has loosened the dentition, experience significant tooth wear from grinding or acid erosion, or have a collapsed or painful bite. Patients with failing old crowns, bridges or dentures that need complete redesign are also typical. Before approving treatment, Korean clinics assess overall oral and general health. Conditions such as uncontrolled diabetes, heavy smoking, certain bone-density issues or active infection can affect implant success and may need to be managed first. Adequate jawbone is important for implants; where bone is insufficient, grafting or alternative designs may be planned. Candidates should be willing to commit to a staged plan that unfolds over several months and more than one trip to Korea, and to maintain diligent home care and follow-up afterward. A thorough consultation, including 3D imaging and a bite analysis, determines whether someone is suitable and which mix of implants, crowns, bridges or alignment best fits their case. Realistic expectations and good communication with the treating team are essential to a successful outcome.
sections.procedure_steps: Treatment generally moves through three broad phases. The first is assessment and planning, where the dental team takes digital X-rays, CBCT scans, intraoral scans and impressions, evaluates gum health and analyzes the bite. From this they build a detailed, written treatment plan that lists every procedure, its sequence and the expected timeline. The second is the preparatory phase, which can include professional cleaning, periodontal therapy to control gum disease, extraction of teeth that are too damaged to save, and bone grafting where the jaw needs reinforcement before implants. The third is the restorative phase. Here, titanium implant posts are surgically placed into the jawbone to act as artificial roots, and a healing period allows them to fuse with the bone. Crowns are then made to cover and protect weakened teeth, while bridges replace gaps by anchoring to neighboring teeth or implants. Custom abutments and final crowns, frequently produced with CAD/CAM technology, are fitted to complete the rehabilitation. Temporary restorations are often provided so patients can function between stages. The exact order and combination depend on each patient's needs, and the team adjusts the plan as healing progresses. Because several disciplines are involved, full mouth reconstruction is usually led by a prosthodontist coordinating with surgeons and other specialists.
sections.recovery_timeline: Recovery from full mouth reconstruction is staged rather than continuous, and the multi-visit reality is important to understand before traveling. Simpler cases focused on crowns or limited bridges may be completed over a few weeks to a few months, while moderate cases involving multiple implants or extensive crown work commonly take around four to six months. Complex cases that require bone grafting or full-arch restoration can span six months to a year. The main reason for this length is osseointegration, the process by which implants fuse with the jawbone, which typically needs several months of healing before final crowns are attached. For international patients this almost always means more than one trip to Korea. A frequent pattern is an initial visit for extractions, grafting and implant placement, followed by a healing period spent at home, then a return visit months later for the final restorations once the implants have integrated. Some plans involve additional intermediate trips. Between visits, clinics often provide temporary teeth and conduct remote follow-up by video to monitor progress. After each surgical stage, patients can expect some swelling, tenderness and a soft-food diet for a period, with detailed aftercare instructions from the team. Planning travel, accommodation and time off around these stages is a key part of preparing for treatment in Korea.
sections.cost_breakdown: Costs vary widely with the number of implants, the extent of grafting and the materials chosen, so figures should be treated as approximate ranges rather than quotes. In South Korea, a single dental implant is often quoted in the region of roughly 600 to 1,500 US dollars including the post, abutment and crown, while individual crowns commonly fall around 200 to 600 US dollars each. A full-arch implant solution may be quoted in the order of about 9,000 to 18,000 US dollars per arch under package pricing, and a complete two-arch full mouth reconstruction is frequently estimated in the range of roughly 15,000 to 30,000 US dollars or more depending on complexity. Additional procedures such as bone grafting, sinus lifts, periodontal therapy or orthodontic alignment add to the total. These prices are generally well below comparable treatment in the United States and many Western European countries, which is a major reason patients consider Korea. International patients should also budget separately for flights, accommodation across multiple trips, local transport, and any pre-treatment or follow-up care at home. Reputable Korean clinics typically provide an itemized written estimate after the initial assessment so patients can see how implants, restorations and supporting procedures contribute to the overall figure before committing.
sections.why_korea: South Korea has become a prominent destination for complex dental rehabilitation, supported by a dense and highly competitive dental sector concentrated in Seoul and especially the Gangnam district, a well-known hub for dental and cosmetic care. The country's official medical tourism framework is coordinated by the Korea Health Industry Development Institute, known as KHIDI, which runs the Medical Korea program and publishes information on registered hospitals and certified providers, helping international patients identify vetted clinics. Intense local competition pushes Korean clinics to adopt advanced digital dentistry, including intraoral 3D scanners, CBCT imaging, computer-guided implant placement and CAD/CAM milling for custom abutments and crowns. These tools support precise planning and faster turnaround, which is valuable when treatment must fit into limited trips. Many Seoul clinics also emphasize strict infection-control protocols and pursue recognized accreditation. For patients undertaking a staged full mouth reconstruction, this concentration of expertise, technology and supporting services, often including airport assistance and remote follow-up, makes coordinating a multi-trip plan more manageable. Combined with costs that are typically lower than in many Western markets, these factors explain why Seoul and Gangnam frequently feature in patients' decisions to pursue full mouth reconstruction in Korea, while official resources such as KHIDI and Medical Korea help with verifying providers.

faqs.q: How many trips to Korea will I need for a full mouth reconstruction?
faqs.a: Most international patients need at least two trips. A common pattern is an initial visit for extractions, any bone grafting and implant placement, then a healing period of several months at home, followed by a return visit for the final crowns or bridges once the implants have fused with the bone. More complex cases can involve additional intermediate visits, and clinics often use video follow-up between trips.

faqs.q: How much does full mouth reconstruction cost in Korea?
faqs.a: It depends heavily on how many implants and restorations are needed and whether grafting is required, so any figure is approximate. As a rough guide, single implants are often around 600 to 1,500 US dollars and crowns around 200 to 600 US dollars each, with complete two-arch reconstructions frequently estimated in the range of about 15,000 to 30,000 US dollars or more. A clinic provides an itemized estimate after the initial assessment.

faqs.q: Why does full mouth reconstruction take several months?
faqs.a: The main reason is osseointegration, the process by which titanium implants fuse with the jawbone. This usually needs several months of healing before final restorations can be attached. Preparatory steps such as treating gum disease, extractions and bone grafting also need their own healing time, which is why the overall plan is staged rather than completed in a single appointment.

faqs.q: What technology do Korean clinics use for full mouth reconstruction?
faqs.a: Many Korean clinics use digital dentistry tools such as intraoral 3D scanners, CBCT imaging, computer-guided implant placement and CAD/CAM milling to design and produce custom abutments and crowns. These technologies support precise planning and efficient turnaround, which is helpful when treatment must be coordinated around a limited number of trips.

faqs.q: Will I have teeth between my visits to Korea?
faqs.a: In most cases yes. Clinics commonly provide temporary restorations or provisional teeth so that you can speak, eat softer foods and maintain appearance while implants heal and final restorations are being prepared. Your treatment plan will explain what interim solution you will have during each healing stage.

references.label: Medical Korea (official medical tourism portal, registered hospitals)
